content:
{
  "title": "Self-Consistent Chain-of-Guard Prompts for Adversarial Robustness in LLMs",
  "problem": "Large language models are vulnerable to prompt-based adversarial attacks and jailbreak attempts that bypass safety filters. Existing static prompt defenses are often circumvented by cleverly crafted or novel attack prompts.",
  "motivation": "Ensuring LLM safety is critical as they are increasingly integrated into sensitive applications. Recent jailbreak incidents show that adversaries can easily adapt to fixed prompt defenses, necessitating more dynamic and robust prompting paradigms. Leveraging LLMs' own reasoning and consistency abilities offers an underexplored path to improve robustness.",
  "method": "We propose 'Chain-of-Guard' prompting, which interleaves user queries with multi-stage, self-consistency safety checks generated by the model itself. For each input, the system generates multiple paraphrases and adversarial variants of the prompt using the LLM, then runs them through a safety filter prompt chain. The outputs are cross-checked for semantic consistency and safety violations; if discrepancies or unsafe outputs are detected, the model abstains or triggers an escalation. This dynamic, adversarially-aware chain increases resistance to prompt-based attacks via internal redundancy and self-evaluation.",
  "experiment_plan": "We will evaluate this method on jailbreak/adversarial prompt datasets such as AdvBench, RealToxicityPrompts, and custom attack suites. Baselines include vanilla prompting, static safety prompts, and recent adversarial training methods. Metrics will include attack success rate, false positive/negative rates, and latency overhead, measuring both robustness and practicality."
}
